title: Weather & Seasons
shortSummary: Talk about the heat, the rains, and typhoons, the rhythms of Philippine weather.
culturalNote: The Philippines is tropical, so instead of four seasons there are mainly two: tag-init (the hot, dry season, roughly March to May) and tag-ulan (the rainy season, June to November). The hottest weeks before the rains arrive are so intense that locals simply call it summer, and schools schedule their long break then. The rainy season brings bagyo (typhoons), which can be powerful, so weather updates and storm signals are taken seriously, and classes are often suspended (walang pasok) for safety. Filipinos handle the heat with cold halo-halo, electric fans, and afternoon naps, and greet sudden downpours with a dash for cover and a shared laugh. Talking about the weather, especially Ang init! (So hot!), is a universal icebreaker.

grammar.rule: Describing weather with the prefix ma-
grammar.explanation: The prefix ma- turns a noun into a quality: araw (sun) becomes maaraw (sunny), ulan (rain) becomes maulan (rainy), lamig (cold) becomes malamig (cold). Use these to describe the weather as an adjective.
grammar.commonMistake: Forgetting the ma- prefix and using the bare noun, such as araw ngayon instead of maaraw ngayon.

grammar.explanation: To exclaim 'how / so + adjective', use Ang plus the adjective: Ang init! (So hot!), Ang ganda! (So beautiful!). This is the most common way Filipinos react to the weather and to things around them.
grammar.commonMistake: Adding the linker -ng after ang in this pattern; it stays ang init, not anging init.

dialogue.setup: Neighbours Ditas and Boy talk over the fence as clouds gather.

lines.speaker: Ditas
lines.native: Naku, ang init kanina, ngayon umuulan na!
lines.english: Oh my, it was so hot earlier, now it's raining!

lines.speaker: Boy
lines.native: Oo nga. May bagyo raw na paparating.
lines.english: Yes indeed. They say a typhoon is coming.

lines.speaker: Ditas
lines.native: Talaga? Mabuti nag-impake na ako ng pagkain.
lines.english: Really? Good thing I already stocked up on food.

lines.speaker: Boy
lines.native: Tama ka. Baka may baha sa kalsada mamaya.
lines.english: You're right. There might be flooding on the road later.

lines.speaker: Ditas
lines.native: Walang pasok daw bukas ang mga bata.
lines.english: They say there are no classes for the kids tomorrow.

lines.speaker: Boy
lines.native: Mabuti, mas ligtas sa bahay. Mag-ingat ka, Ditas.
lines.english: Good, safer at home. Take care, Ditas.

lines.speaker: Ditas
lines.native: Ikaw rin, Boy. Magdala ka ng payong palagi!
lines.english: You too, Boy. Always bring an umbrella!

quiz.question: How do you say 'It's so hot today!' in Filipino?
quiz.explanation: Ang init! is the classic exclamation for hot weather.

quiz.question: What does 'tag-ulan' mean?
quiz.explanation: Tag-ulan is the rainy season; tag-init is the hot, dry season.

quiz.question: What does 'walang pasok' usually mean during a typhoon?
quiz.explanation: Walang pasok means classes or work are suspended for safety.

quiz.question: Arrange: payong / ka / ng / magdala
quiz.explanation: Magdala ka ng payong means 'Bring an umbrella'.
